main_summary.summary: Lonnie C. King, founding chair of the Atlanta Committee on Appeal for Human Rights (COAHR) and co-leader of the Atlanta Student Movement, recounts his life from his 1936 birth in Arlington, Georgia, through his 1960 leadership of the AU Center student campaign that integrated downtown Atlanta. He describes being raised in Calhoun County by his grandfather Rev. Joseph P. Smith, an NAACP-recruiting Baptist evangelist; his mother's move bringing him to Atlanta at age eight; his formation at Ebenezer Baptist Church under Martin Luther King Sr. and at the Butler Street YMCA; and his three-year Navy enlistment, where he confronted segregated billet assignments aboard the USS Roanoke. After returning to Morehouse in 1957 as a football fullback, the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-ins prompted him to organize with Joseph Pierce and Julian Bond. He details the AU Council of Presidents meeting where Rufus E. Clement, Benjamin E. Mays, Albert E. Manley, James P. Brawley counseled restraint and Harry V. Richardson and the Morris Brown president broke ranks; the drafting of An Appeal for Human Rights by Roslyn Pope; the May 17, 1960 march; the boycott targeting Rich's department store; the Shaw University conference founding SNCC; the October 1960 jailing with Martin Luther King Jr.; and the March 10, 1961 Warren Memorial Methodist Church mass meeting where the lunch-counter desegregation agreement was ratified.
key_themes: Lunch-counter boycott of Rich's department store
AU Council of Presidents and Black institutional politics
main_summary.historical_significance:
King's testimony is a first-person leadership account of the Atlanta Student Movement from inside its founding committee, covering organizational decisions rarely documented elsewhere: the AU Council of Presidents meeting at Harkness Hall, Roslyn Pope's authorship of the Appeal, the M. Kasuth Hill financing of the Atlanta Inquirer as a movement-aligned counter to C.A. Scott's Atlanta Daily World, the walkie-talkie communications system developed by Ronald Yancey for boycott coordination, and the Boynton v. Virginia test rides Atlanta students conducted months before CORE's 1961 Freedom Rides. King also gives one of the few firsthand accounts of the March 10, 1961 mass meeting at Warren Memorial Methodist Church where Martin Luther King Jr. salvaged the disputed downtown-desegregation agreement after the community booed Daddy King.

The interview's value to the historical record is its corrective specificity on contested points: King names Diane Nash as the actual leader of the Nashville movement whom Marion Barry and James Bevel leapfrogged; he places himself at the SNCC founding meeting at Ella Baker's invitation alongside Otis Moss Jr.; and he identifies Frank H. Neely (Rich's chairman) and John C. Calhoun (former Atlanta NAACP head) as the figures who persuaded him to accept the negotiated settlement.

chapters.title: Early Years in Arlington, Georgia
chapters.summary: King describes his birth in Arlington in Calhoun County, his mother Bertha Lee Smith King, his parents' divorce when he was two, and the six years he spent with his maternal grandfather Rev. Joseph P. Smith, a Baptist evangelist and NAACP membership recruiter. He recounts the 1942 Eugene Talmadge gubernatorial-campaign leaflet that introduced him to the word 'nigger' and his grandfather's explanation of post-slavery oppression.
related_events: NAACP membership organizing in rural Georgia

chapters.title: Atlanta, Ebenezer, and the Butler Street YMCA
chapters.summary: After his grandfather's death King moves to Atlanta at age eight to live with his mother. He joins Ebenezer Baptist Church during a 1945 spring revival led by Martin Luther King Sr., meets the King family, forms the Ebenezer Youth Organization, and is taken to the Butler Street YMCA by his mother, who arranges with director J.D. Winston to keep watch over him. King describes growing up among the Black movers-and-shakers of Atlanta who frequented the Y.

chapters.title: Navy Service and Confronting Racism
chapters.summary: King enlists in the Navy in 1954 on a Kitty Cruise enlistment after Morehouse tuition becomes unaffordable. Aboard the USS Roanoke he is the only Black sailor of twenty assigned from boot camp, billeted to the deck force chipping paint and cleaning the head despite holding the group's highest GCT score. He recounts the chaplain's refusal to advocate for him, Lt. J.G. Horn's intervention, and Welty Wolf and John Bloom teaching him disbursing-clerk skills so he could pass the petty-officer exam.

chapters.title: Greensboro, Morehouse, and Organizing the AU Center
chapters.summary: Back at Morehouse in 1957 as a starting fullback, King reads the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-in news at Yates and Milton drug store and immediately recruits Joseph Pierce and Julian Bond. He convenes the first student meeting at Morehouse with about twenty attendees and brings in James Felder from Clark College and football teammates. Within days Mrs. Hill, Dr. Mays's secretary, summons him, Pierce, and Bond to the AU Council of Presidents at Harkness Hall.

chapters.title: Council of Presidents and An Appeal for Human Rights
chapters.summary: King recounts the Harkness Hall meeting where Rufus E. Clement, Benjamin E. Mays, Albert E. Manley, and James P. Brawley counseled students to defer to the NAACP, while Harry V. Richardson of the Interdenominational Theological Center and Frank Cunningham of Morris Brown broke ranks to support the students. Clement proposed and funded a published manifesto. Roslyn Pope drafted An Appeal for Human Rights, typed at Howard Zinn's house, published March 9, 1960 in the Atlanta Constitution and Journal; it was reprinted in the New York Times, The Nation, and Harvard Crimson, and entered into the Congressional Record by Sen. Jacob Javits.
related_events: March 9 1960 publication of An Appeal for Human Rights

chapters.title: The May 17 March and Mobilizing Spelman
chapters.summary: King describes the difficulty of expanding beyond thirty to fifty participants until his mother advised him to organize Spelman women, after which Morehouse, Clark, and Morris Brown men followed. He recounts debating Roy Wilkins at Spelman in April 1960 alongside Manley, deploying the rhetoric 'if not now, when; if not us, who.' On May 17, 1960, the sixth anniversary of Brown v. Board, roughly 4,000 students marched from the AU campus toward the Georgia Capitol; Gov. Ernest Vandiver had deployed state troopers, and King, after consulting Mays and Chief Herbert Jenkins, turned the march at Whitehall Street to avoid violence.
related_events: Brown v. Board sixth anniversary commemoration

chapters.title: Mann Brothers Acid Attack and Tactical Nonviolence
chapters.summary: King describes the boycott of Mann Brothers grocery store in West End where a white man doused him with an acid-like substance that destroyed his shirt and burned his skin. He notes that the Atlanta Student Movement adopted nonviolence as a tactic rather than a way of life, did not run formal nonviolence workshops as Nashville did, and that he warned recruits at the May 17 march that anyone unable to absorb blows or be spat on should not march.
related_events: Atlanta vs Nashville nonviolence training contrast

chapters.title: The Atlanta Inquirer and the Richard Rich Confrontation
chapters.summary: King describes his June 1960 confrontation with Richard Rich in Chief Herbert Jenkins's office at City Hall after the Magnolia Room arrest, and Rich's threat that King would not return downtown. He recounts M. Kasuth Hill, son of Atlanta's first Black physician, walking into his office with the Atlanta Daily World and proposing to found the Atlanta Inquirer using his offset press, William Strong as attorney, and Julian Bond and the Gibson brothers as writers. The Inquirer launched July 31, 1960, after C.A. Scott of the Atlanta Daily World threatened King over a $9,000 colonial-stores advertising contract.

chapters.title: Shaw Conference and the Founding of SNCC
chapters.summary: King describes proposing to Martin Luther King Jr. that he convene a region-wide student-leadership meeting; King contributed $500 toward the gathering, and Ella Baker arranged for Shaw University to host it over Easter weekend 1960. King argues with Baker, who favored loose grassroots coordination, for a formal organization on the grounds that the segregationist apparatus was already organized state-by-state. He names the Atlanta delegation (Julian Bond, Ben Brown, Marion Barry's election as temporary chair, Marian Wright Edelman, Johnny Parham) and assesses Diane Nash as the actual Nashville leader leapfrogged by Marion Barry and James Bevel.

chapters.title: Boynton v. Virginia Test and the October 1960 Jailing
chapters.summary: King recounts the Atlanta Student Movement's Boynton v. Virginia test in December 1960, teams to Chattanooga (Maurice Pennington and Morris Dillard), Jacksonville (Norma June Wilson), Birmingham (Marion Wright), and Columbia, S.C., months before CORE's 1961 Freedom Rides; James Farmer later acknowledged the precedent. He then describes the October 19, 1960 Rich's department store sit-in he persuaded Martin Luther King Jr. to join despite Jr.'s probation from the DeKalb County traffic case, citing Daddy King's own sermon 'you can't lead from the back.' The walkie-talkie communications system was developed by Ronald Yancey, the first Black graduate of Georgia Tech.

chapters.title: The March 10 Mass Meeting at Warren Memorial
chapters.summary: King describes being summoned by Jesse Hill Jr. to a Chamber of Commerce meeting where the white merchants and Black old-guard leaders presented the negotiated agreement to desegregate downtown lunch counters after school desegregation in the fall. Frank H. Neely (Rich's chairman) defended King's right to consult his constituency; John C. Calhoun and Rufus E. Clement urged acceptance. Ivan Allen Jr. held an unauthorized press conference, igniting community fury. The March 10, 1961 mass meeting at Rev. Stinson's Warren Memorial Methodist Church drew over 2,000 inside plus crowds outside; after the community booed Martin Luther King Sr., Martin Luther King Jr. delivered what King calls the most powerful speech of his career and salvaged the agreement.

chapters.title: Reflections on the Movement's Legacy
chapters.summary: King reflects on the 1960s civil rights movement as the country's most consequential social transformation since the end of slavery, opening Title VII protections that benefited women, Latinos, and gays. He critiques contemporary African American economic stagnation, citing personal responsibility alongside structural inequity, and tells the story of a Spelman scholarship recipient who had been studying by cell-phone light in an Atlanta homeless shelter as evidence that long-distance-runner resilience still matters.
related_events: Civil Rights Act of 1964 Title VII passage

loc_video.caption: Lonnie C. King oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Atlanta, Georgia, 2013 May 29
